figures.caption: Table 1: Comparison of VLA inference methods on LIBERO. Success rate (%, mean across 3 random seeds × 20 episodes per task) across four suites, average per-step latency (averaged over VLM and non-VLM steps within one call period f ), average episode length on successful episodes, and net wall-clock speedup. Feature Caching (∆t = 0) reuses stale VLM outputs at matched f without learned prediction. Full FastV and VLA-Cache adaptation details (layer selection, prune/threshold sweeps) are in Appendix H.
Table 7: Per-task success rates on RoboCasa (GR00T, zero-shot bridge, 20 episodes/task, f=3). Bridge retains 95.4% of sync SR on average, matching sync or exceeding it on 9 of 24 tasks.

Figure 6: LIBERO-Spatial (pick up the black bowl next to the plate and place it on the plate): trajectory comparison. Row 1 (Sync): VLM runs every step—task succeeds. Row 2 (Bridge): KV deltas predicted by Latent Bridge (f=3)—task succeeds with near-identical trajectory. Row 3 (Feature Cache): stale KV reused without update—robot deviates and fails.
Figure 7: LIBERO-Spatial Task 8: KV cache cosine similarity to ground truth over one episode.
Table 6: Per-task success counts (out of 20 episodes) for the π0.5 KV bridge at f=4 across the four LIBERO suites. Suite averages (bottom row) match Table 1.

Table 2: Cross-benchmark results at f=3 (all values are success rate in %, mean across 3 seeds). RoboCasa: 24 kitchen tasks, GR00T, zero-shot. ALOHA: transfer-cube, π0.5, 50 episodes per seed, 1-step denoise.

Figure 2: Architecture comparison. Both variants use a DiT backbone with AdaLN conditioning. GR00T operates on a single feature vector; π0.5 operates on per-layer KV pairs. Zero-initialized output ensures the bridge starts at the copy baseline.
Figure 8: LIBERO-Object (pick up the ketchup and place it in the basket): trajectory comparison. Bridge produces a near-identical trajectory to Sync; Feature Cache deviates due to stale KV.
Figure 9: LIBERO-Object Task 4: KV cache cosine similarity to ground truth over one episode.
Table 5: Bridge model hyperparameters. The small variant achieves comparable SR; we use the full variant in all main experiments.

Figure 5: Case study on a LIBERO-Spatial episode at f=3. (a) KV cosine to ground truth: caching (red) drops to ∼0.91 in a sawtooth pattern; bridge (blue) stays at ∼0.99. (b) Keyframe comparison: caching drifts off-trajectory and fails; bridge tracks the sync trajectory and succeeds.
Figure 11: LIBERO-10 Task 4: KV cache cosine similarity to ground truth over one episode.

Figure 10: LIBERO-10 (put the white mug on the left plate and put the yellow and white mug on the right plate): trajectory comparison. This long-horizon, multi-step task highlights Bridge’s ability to maintain KV fidelity over extended episodes.

Figure 3: Task-agnostic three-stage pipeline. The same pipeline transfers across all LIBERO suites, RoboCasa, and ALOHA sim without modification. DAgger closes the distribution gap between sync training and bridge deployment.

Table 11: Per-component inference latency (ms, median). GR00T runs a lightweight feature bridge with no vision encoder on bridge steps; π0.5 runs SigLIP every step to provide fresh embedding input to the KV bridge.
